observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to immediately record in the “Accounts Receivable” the total unpaid lease/rental fees of delinquent lessees of market stalls as of December 31, 2024 totaling to ₱ 41,375.00. She must furnish the audit team with certified copies of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and other supporting documents for verification.
The Municipal Economic Enterprise Development Officer to compute the surcharges of the unpaid lease/rental fees of market stalls which accumulated to ₱ 41,375.00 and provide a report to the Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV so she can record  them in the books as additional “Accounts Receivable” of the LGU. Furnish the audit team with the JEV and other pertinent documentary supports for corroboration.
The Municipal Economic Enterprise Development Officer to direct the MEEDO collectors to expedite the collection of the unpaid lease/rental fees, including their surcharges, from the delinquent lessees. They must be collected on or before the end of the first quarter of 2025, otherwise, they should not be allowed to renew their contracts of lease.
The Municipal Economic Enterprise Development Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Mayor’s Office personnel to secure the copies of the 267 contracts of lease that were executed last January 2024 from the lessees. Submit certified copies to COA to continue with our audit.
Hereafter, the Municipal Economic Enterprise Development Officer must initiate to renew the 267 contracts of lease considering the one year lease term per Ordinance No. 14 Series of 2023. The stipulations therein must include the imposition of surcharges in case of non-payment of lease/rent on time among others. Additionally, he must keep copies of the contracts of lease as bases for the collections of lease/rental fees of market stalls.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to do the following courses of action:
To prepare adjusting entries to properly record the land improvements that were erroneously recognized and recorded as “Land Improvements-Aquaculture Structures” to their correct asset accounts duly substantiated with proper documentary supports and furnish the audit team with the same for verification.
To derecognize the land improvements with minimal amounts which aggregated to ₱ 613,183.88 that should not be capitalized. The Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) must be duly supported with pertinent documents and submit the same to COA for evaluation.
Hereafter, to clearly check and closely examine the nature of the disbursement first before recording it in the books of accounts of the LGU to prevent similar deficiency in the future.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to immediately prepare adjusting entries to derecognize the costs of the demolished structures and their corresponding accumulated depreciation from the books of accounts of the LGU.
Further, the Municipal Accountant must prepare the depreciation schedule of these properties as of December 31, 2024 and submit it together with other pertinent documents to COA for evaluation.
The GSO-Designate to immediately submit the inventory reports of the scrap materials from the five (5) demolished structures to the audit team for review and verification.  He must include in submitting the Property Transfer Report (PTR) for scrap materials already transferred to the barangay governments of the Municipality of Kibawe.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Administrative Officer IV-OIC/Municipal Accountant-Designate must immediately locate or dig in the documents pertaining to the two (2) unrecorded construction and heavy equipment to determine their acquisition costs and prepare adjusting entries to account them all in the books of accounts of the LGU duly substantiated with complete documentary supports. She must furnish certified copies of the same to the audit team for verification. Further, she must also consider their depreciation expenses in recording and submit depreciation schedule to the audit team for verification.
The Administrative Officer IV-OIC or the Municipal Accountant-Designate must prepare adjusting entry relative to the repairs that were capitalized which aggregated to ₱ 475,550.00 duly supported with complete documentary requirements. Additionally, adjust their depreciation expenses as well if there are any. Submit certified copies of the same to COA for evaluation.
The Administrative Officer IV-OIC or the Municipal Accountant-Designate must trace the nature of the negative or abnormal balance of the equipment (Item 12, Table-1) amounted to ₱ 486,434.18. Prepare adjusting entries if necessary to reflect the proper acquisition cost and accumulated depreciation of the said equipment. Submit the certified copies and documentary supports to the audit team for evaluation.
The Municipal Engineer must immediately update its inventory report of the construction and heavy equipment and include Items 9, 10 & 12 as presented in the foregoing table.
The Municipal Engineer must issue Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) for all the construction and heavy equipment under his custody and owned by LGU-Kibawe. Submit copies to COA for reference and as compliance to this AOM. Further, maintain master files of these PARs for reference.
Hereafter, the Administrative Officer IV-OIC/Municipal Accountant-Designate must regularly reconcile its records with the Municipal Engineering Office to prevent the same deficiency in the future.
Henceforth, the Municipal Engineer to prepare Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) every time a new construction and heavy equipment is issued to the Municipal Engineering Office in order to easily identify the accountable officer of the said equipment.
Hereafter, the management must immediately conduct one-time cleansing of the “Construction and Heavy Equipment” account as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to do the following courses of action:
To immediately prepare journal entry voucher (JEV) to adjust the rehabilitation/repair projects amounted to ₱ 310,836.00 that were booked under “Construction in Progress-Infrastructure Assets” account and furnish the audit team with certified copies of the same for review and verification.
To review and trace all the other disbursements related to Projects No. 1, 2 & 5 that were not accounted in “CIP-Infrastructure Assets” account and ensure that they were not capitalized. Prepare adjusting entries if they were capitalized duly supported with pertinent documents and submit the same to COA for corroboration.
Hereafter, to closely examine the nature of the project first before recording them in books of accounts of the LGU to prevent similar deficiency in the future.
Henceforth, to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer as to the monitoring of the completion of the LGU projects booked in the “CIP” accounts by asking or securing from the latter the pertinent Certificates of Project Completion regularly.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to do the following courses of action:
To immediately adjust the “Loans Receivable” and “Loans Payable” account and correct the overstatement amounting to ₱ 17,656,183.09 duly substantiated with pertinent documents. Submit the same to the audit team for review and verification.
Hereafter, to record “Loans Payable-Domestic” only upon receipt of the actual loan proceeds from the creditor/bank.
Henceforth, to carefully review the prescribed Chart of Accounts (COA Circular No. 2015-009)  particularly their definitions and usage before recording in the LGU’s books of accounts to prevent similar deficiency in the future.

We recommended to the OIC-Administrative Officer IV to adjust the interest income amounted to ₱ 29,841.91 recorded in the SEF book and ₱ 25,646.08 accounted in the TF booked under the “Other Payables” account by accounting them as part of the General Fund and by crediting “Prior Period Adjustments”. She must submit certified copies of the journal entry voucher (JEV) and pertinent documents to the audit team for verification.
Henceforth, the OIC-Administrative Officer IV must immediately recognize and classify the interest income as general income under the General Fund in the ensuing years.

We recommended to the OIC-Administrative IV/Municipal Accountant-Designate to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer in preparing the required disbursement voucher, Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and other essential supporting documents such as but not limited to the copy of the subsidiary ledger pertaining to the unutilized PDAF. Furnish the audit team with copies of the same for verification. Once set, the Municipal Treasurer must immediately issue a check payable to the Bureau of the Treasury amounted to ₱ 69,000.00.

We recommended to the LSB Chairperson to direct the LSB Secretary to do the following courses of action:
Provide the Local Accountant and Local Treasurer their copies of the SEF budget strictly within the indicated timeframe.
Formally clarify the submission process with the DepEd CO to have a clear understanding of where and how the quarterly SEF utilization reports should be submitted. Also, ensure the regular submission of the reports to the DepEd CO once the process is clarified
Furnish the local sanggunian and DBM regional office their copy of the quarterly SEF utilization reports to adhere with the requirements of Section 6.1 of DepEd DBM DILG JC 2017-01.
Obtain and submit to COA a circular, guideline or letter from the DILG confirming that posting the SEF utilization reports on the FDPP is sufficient in lieu of physically furnishing a copy to the DILG regional office. If such a circular, guideline or letter is not available, the LSB must comply with the requirement to physically furnish the report.
Ensure that the submission of reports and furnishing of its copies to the intended recipients, are properly supported with stamped transmittal letters confirming receipt.

We recommended to the management to submit a clear and legal basis or the like for the payment of the “Yuletide Social Assistance” to the audit team to continue with our post audit of the said transaction. Otherwise, immediately refund the ₱ 66,000.00.
Henceforth, the LGU must stop granting allowances to barangay officials without clear and legal basis to prevent the audit team from issuing audit suspension or disallowance.

We recommended to the management to immediately submit to the audit team the noted lacking documents to substantiate the disbursements and for the audit team to continue with the post-audit of the transactions.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action.
The Municipal Mayor to:
Submit to the audit team the lacking supporting documents enumerated in the Roman Numeral No. I and II of this AOM, including a travel itinerary that includes the computation of DSA as outlined in Section 14 of EO 77, for further review.
Direct the Municipal Vice Mayor to submit to the audit team the lacking supporting documents for his foreign travel enumerated in the Roman Numeral No. I and II of this AOM, including a revised travel itinerary that includes the computation of DSA as outlined in Section 14 of EO 77, for further review.
The management to stop, if not, minimize foreign travels and evaluate future foreign travels with thorough cost-benefit analysis and consider alternative options that minimize government spending while achieving the same objectives. Strictly observe prudence and judiciousness in the utilization of public funds.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to adjust the “Accounts Payable” account as to the remaining unpaid mandatory contributions, claims of officers and employees, barangay financial assistance and fund transfer upon receipt of this AOM to their proper liability accounts in the General Fund (GF) and Special Education Fund (SEF) books duly substantiated with complete documentary supports and furnish the audit team with the same for verification.
Hereafter, the Municipal Accountant-Designate/OIC-Administrative Officer IV to closely examine the nature of the unpaid disbursement before recording it in the books of accounts of the LGU.
Henceforth, management must refrain from resorting to reimbursements and must opt to proper procurement process instead.

We recommended to the management to direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to identify the payees of the staled checks and verify the status if the payees still have valid claims. Additionally, the Municipal Accountant should immediately prepare adjusting entries for the stale checks that have valid claims by debiting “Cash in Bank-LCCA” and crediting “Account Payable” accounts. Likewise, if the payee has no valid claims, the Accountant shall record the transactions as debit “Cash in Bank-LCCA” and credit “Prior Period Adjustment” accounts. Moreover, submit the certified copy of the JEV of the recorded adjustment along with the confirmation letter of the payee to the office of the auditor.

Also, include the data of the names of the payees on outstanding checks for monitoring purposes. 

Henceforth, the Acting Municipal Accountant should record stale checks in accordance with Section 61 Item 6 of the NGAS Manual to accurately present the “Cash in Bank” account and its corresponding affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Confirmation letters were already sent to the identified payees.
observations.reason: Management didn’t know the exact address of some of the payees and was not able to locate them.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The Municipal Treasurer must immediately update the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card by considering the assessment roll provided by the Municipal Assessor in CY 2022. He then prepares the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for CY 2023 and furnishes the Acting Municipal Accountant. From there, the Acting Municipal Accountant must prepare adjusting entries if there are overstatements/understatements in the RPT and SET Receivables already established and recorded in CY 2023. The RPT and SET Receivables must always be even. She must also furnish the Office of the Auditor with the certified copies of the journal entry vouchers, subsidiary ledgers and other pertinent documents. 

The Acting Municipal Accountant must also immediately reconcile the beginning balances of the RPT/SET Receivables with a variance amounted to ₱ 1.06 million. Ideally, they must have analogous amounts at the beginning of CY 2023. She must prepare the necessary adjusting entries with complete supporting documents and furnish the Office of the Auditor with their certified copies.  

Hereafter, the Municipal Treasurer must prepare and provide the Acting Municipal Accountant with the duly certified lists showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for establishing and recording the current RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The Acting Municipal Accountant must issue a written demand letter addressed to the District Engineer (DE) of Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) - Bukidnon Second District Engineering Office for the immediate submission of the copies of Fund Utilization Reports, Journal of Checks Issued (only related to the paid disbursement vouchers) duly received by COA-DPWH and certified copies of the Journal Entry Voucher from DPWH taking up the disbursements of ₱ 1.7 million LGU counterparts. Once obtained, she must immediately prepare an adjusting entry relative to the ₱ 1.7 million and submit the certified copies of the journal entry voucher, subsidiary ledger and other pertinent documents to the Office of the Auditor. 

2. Hereafter, management must always keep a copy of an approved Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) for every LGU counterpart to National Government Agencies (NGAs).

We recommended to the Acting Municipal Accountant the following courses of action:

1. To start gathering data and establish the important details related to the foregoing Property, Plant and Equipment items particularly the time when it was acquired or purchased for every equipment, or date of completion for each infrastructure project accounted in land improvements, aquaculture structures and school building accounts;

2. To religiously prepare their lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component at the end of the year; and

3.  Hereafter, to start recognizing and recording accumulated depreciation and depreciation expense at the end of every year pertaining to said PPE accounts.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to reclassify the non-current portion of ‘Loan’s Payable - Domestic’ account indicating the current and non-current portion of the said liabilities and disclose the significant information of the account to present fairly the financial statements.

We also recommended to the OIC - Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to submit to this office copies of the amortization schedule, Loan Agreement and Notice of Loan Approval once retrieved for further verification.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

1. The Acting Municipal Accountant must first secure the Certificate of Project Acceptance and Turn-over attested by the Punong Barangay and Inspection Report pertaining to the said Rehabilitation project from the barangay management of Balintawak and submit certified copies to the Office of the Auditor. 

2. The Acting Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer must facilitate the transfer of the unexpended LGU counterpart of the fully completed project in the amount of ₱ 1 million from the Trust Fund to the General Fund once the required documentary requirements have already been obtained.

3.  Management must revert the ₱ 1 million to unappropriated surplus or as one of the sources of funds for a supplemental appropriation of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: The MACCO was still coordinating with the Punong Barangay of the recipient barangay related to the required certificate.
observations.reason: Unexpended counterpart amounted to ₱ 1 million remained in the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to require the GSO to submit a written justification for why the Purchase Requests (PRs) prices per liter under DV Number 101-23-01-66, 101-23-01-143, and 101-23-06-1068 were significantly above the Province of Bukidnon's Prevailing Retail Pump Price issued by the Department of Energy website to the office of the auditor for review and validation. Likewise, require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to attach the certification from the supplier that the price adjustments are those reported and duly posted on DOE’s website.  

Also, we recommended that the LCE ensures that the procurement of bulk purchase of POL should conform to GPPB Resolution No. 26-2019, dated October 30, 2019.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The Municipal Economic Enterprise and Development Officer (MEEDO) must immediately prepare and maintain duly notarized contracts of lease between the 1,278 lessees of the Kibawe Public Cemetery and the Municipal Mayor; 

The MEEDO must directly submit monitoring reports showing the maturity terms of the leases of the 1,278 total lessees of all leased sections of Kibawe Public Cemetery to the Office of the Auditor for corroboration; and
3. The MEEDO must immediately reconcile its report with the subsidiary ledger prepared and maintained by the Municipal Accounting Office in order to address the total variance amounted to ₱ 395,842.00 and submit the reconciling statements, working papers and the like to the Office of the Auditor for review and verification.
observations.managementAction: Partial preparation of the contracts of lease pertaining to the Kibawe Public Cemetery by the MEEDO
observations.reason: Not all contracts were prepared by MEEDO

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

1. To immediately submit the following to the Office of the Auditor to continue with the post audit of transactions:

a. Communication, request letter or the like from Barangay Cabadiangan asking for assistance to LGU Kibawe;
b.    Any documents that would prove that there was an understanding, consent or agreement between LGU Kibawe and the officials of Barangay Cabadiangan, Kadingilan, Bukidnon for the said undertaking; and

c.  An official list of the intended beneficiaries living in Barangay Cabadiangan generated by the Barangay Government Unit of Cabadiangan, Kadingilan.

2.  To immediately reconcile the variances noted which aggregated to ₱ 108,352.89 and submit the reconciling statements, working papers or the like to the Office of the Auditor for review and verification.

3. Hereafter, the General Services Officer (GSO) and Acting Municipal Accountant must observe the correct treatment and proper procedure in handling and distribution of welfare goods to their intended recipients.
observations.managementAction: Management is currently reaching out to Barangay Cabadiangan, Kadingilan relative to the required supporting documents by the audit team.
observations.reason: Lacking documents were not yet submitted by the management to the audit team

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to submit a written justification for hiring a consultant for the application of the PRDP and why was it procured through Highly Technical Consultants under Section 53.7 of the revised IRR of RA 9184. Likewise, submit the lacking documents namely the completely filled-out official receipt to support Disbursement Voucher Number 101-23-05-899, a copy of the approved manning schedule indicating the names and position of the consultants and staff and the extent of their participation in the project, a copy of curriculum vitae of the consultant and staff,  letter request for the payment from the consultant, Progress/Final Billing, the Social Environmental Safeguards, and the Environmental Compliance Certificate to the Office of the Auditor to continue with the post audit of transactions.

Also, we recommended to the management to procure consultancy services through competitive bidding, except as provided in Rule XVI of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The gathering of the required documents is still a work in progress.
observations.reason: Lacking documents were not yet submitted by the management

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

1. The Municipal Health Officer must coordinate with the Acting Municipal Accountant in securing the MOA, guidelines or the like from the management of Southern Bukidnon Inter-Local Health Zone (SBILHZ) relative to LGU Kibawe’s cost sharing and immediately submit it to the Office of the Auditor to continue with the post audit of transaction.

2. Hereafter, the Municipal Treasurer must stop paying the Southern Bukidnon Inter-Local Health Zone (SBILHZ), through the Municipal Treasurer of Maramag, Bukidnon, for the LGU’s cost sharing if the MOA, guidelines or the like cannot be secured by the Municipal Health Officer.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action:

1. To submit a clear and definite legal basis for the payment of the honoraria to the Provincial Legal Officer to the Office of the Auditor to continue with the post audit of the said transactions;

2. Otherwise, to stop the payment of honoraria to the Provincial Legal Officer; and

3. To refrain from hiring a private lawyer for legal matters except in the affairs stated in COA Circular 98-002 dated June 9, 1998.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The Acting Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer must immediately reconcile the variance amounting to ₱ 899,983.84 and submit the reconciling statements, working papers or the like to the Office of the Auditor for review and verification.

After reconciling, the Acting Municipal Accountant must immediately transfer the CY 2023 unexpended balances of QRF and MF - MOOE to the Special Trust Fund in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002 and submit the certified copies of the Journal Entry Voucher, subsidiary ledger and other pertinent documents to the Office of the Auditor. 

Henceforth, the unspent DRRMF which include the unutilized QRF and unexpended DRRMF-MOOE of the current year must be transferred to the STF at the end of the current year - before the agency’s books of accounts are closed, and not in the succeeding year.

We recommended to the Acting Municipal Accountant to:

Transfer the unspent DRRMF which include the unutilized Quick Response Fund and unexpended DRRMF-MOOE of the current year to the Trust Fund book of accounts at the end of the current year and not in the succeeding year; and

Recognize and record the corresponding expense account (Transfer of Unspent Current Year DRRM) and trust liability account (Trust Liability – DRRMF) in the General Fund and Trust Fund books of accounts, respectively, for the current year in order to keep the fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The MDRRMO must prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP including under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged against the unexpended LDRRMF for further verification.

The MPDC must submit the revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) as approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive (LCE) upon recommendation of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) in accordance with the approved LDRRM Plan to support the release and utilization of the unexpended LDRRMF in CY 2023.

Lastly, the MPDC and MDRRMO must ensure that future charges against the unexpended LDRRMF shall be sufficiently supported with (1) LDRRMFIP including under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged against the unexpended LDRRMF, and the (2) revised AIP as approved by the local sanggunian and LCE.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO is still finalizing the revised AIP.
observations.reason: Non-submission of the revised AIP

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following

The Municipal Accountant’s Office make the necessary adjustments on the affected accounts both in the trust fund and general fund; and thereafter, record properties funded out of the financial assistance only when the documents evidencing transfer of ownership could be obtained from the source agency.

The Management to –

Prepare the donation documents and submit the same to the Provincial Monitoring Office so that the donation will be facilitated;
b. Once obtained, forward the Deeds of Donation to the Municipal Accountant’s Office for the recording of the assets in the General Fund of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: The LGU followed up the approved Deeds of Donation from the Provincial Government of Bukidnon
observations.reason: Still waiting for the Deeds of Donation from the Provincial Government of Bukidnon

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The OIC - Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer must immediately submit a legal basis and a written justification for the utilization of 30% Quick Response Fund (QRF) of MDRRMF in CY 2021 to pay the Special Risk Allowance of the LGU’s qualified health workers amounted to ₱ 54,000.00 to the Office of the Auditor for further validation.

The Municipal Health Officer must also submit a written justification for the excessive payment of SRA amounted to ₱ 27,000.00 to COA for additional review and verification; otherwise

Require the eighteen (18) recipients or health workers to refund/return the SRA that they received worth ₱ 54,000.00 to the LGU’s coffer.

observations.managementAction: Management is about to conduct the one-time cleansing of Property, Plant and Equipment.
observations.reason: One PPE account at a time

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to:

Identify the existence of the public infrastructures recorded in the RPI in coordination with the General Service Office (GSO) and Engineering Office;

After identifying the existence of the public infrastructures, prepare the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) through the Accounting Office and make sure to completely fill-up the following information:
Description of the infrastructure asset (type, location, parameters, etc.)
Date of acquisition
Estimated Useful Life in accordance with the guidelines set by the LGU based on its experience
Rate of Depreciation in accordance with existing accounting rules
Residual value in accordance with existing accounting rules
Accumulated Depreciation as calculated based on the given date of acquisition, estimated useful life, rate of depreciation and residual value 

c. After preparing the individual PPELC, record the infrastructure assets in the appropriate infrastructure asset accounts in the books of accounts of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: Covered by the required One Time cleansing of PPE accounts
observations.reason: About to commence with the one time cleansing of PPE

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Management to undertake the steps provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to clean the Supplies Inventory and Property, Plant, and Equipment Accounts of the LGU. 

Once the one-time cleansing is done, we recommended to the Management to:

1.	Instruct the General Services Officer to:
	a. Facilitate the procurement of supplies and PPE by issuing a PR at the beginning of the quarter in accordance with the Annual Procurement Plan of the LGU to avoid unavailability of stocks and consequent delay in the operations of the end-user units;
	b. Secure the Purchase Orders from the BAC and monitor the delivery of the supplies and PPE;
	c. Maintain an updated stock cards for supplies, and property cards for PPE;
	d. Reconcile its records with the records of the Municipal Accountant’s Office; and
	e. Submit to the Office of the Auditor the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) and Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) within the reglementary period of submission.
2.	Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
	a. Maintain an updated Property, Plant and Equipment Cards for each category of assets; and Real Property Ledger Cards for Land; and 
	b. Record and disclose the PPE based on the recognition criteria and disclosure requirement set under IPSAS 17 and the Government Auditing Manual (GAM) for LGUs.
3.	Undertake the annual physical inventory count for the succeeding years.
observations.managementAction: Covered by the required One Time cleansing of PPE accounts
observations.reason: One-time cleansing of PPE is about to commence

observations.managementAction: The MACCO and the Housing Program Coordinator are working hand and hand in order to fully implement the audit recommendations relative to the Housing Program of the LGU.
observations.reason: Non-submission of the pertinent documents that the residential lots and housing units were already fully paid.

We recommended to the Management to undertake the steps provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to clean the Supplies Inventory and Property, Plant, and Equipment Accounts of the LGU. 

Once the one-time cleansing is done, we recommended to the Management to:

1.	Instruct the General Services Officer to:

a.	Facilitate the procurement of supplies and PPE by issuing a PR at the beginning of the quarter in accordance with the Annual Procurement Plan of the LGU to avoid unavailability of stocks and consequent delay in the operations of the end-user units;
b.	Secure the Purchase Orders from the BAC and monitor the delivery of the supplies and PPE;
c.	Maintain an updated stock cards for supplies, and property cards for PPE;
d.	Reconcile its records with the records of the Municipal Accountant’s Office; and
e.	Submit to the Office of the Auditor the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) and Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) within the reglementary period of submission.
2.	Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a.	Maintain an updated Property, Plant and Equipment Cards for each category of assets; and Real Property Ledger Cards for Land; and 
b.	Record and disclose the PPE based on the recognition criteria and disclosure requirement set under IPSAS 17 and the Government Auditing Manual (GAM) for LGUs.
3.	Undertake the annual physical inventory count for the succeeding years.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Management to undertake the steps provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to clean the Supplies Inventory and Property, Plant, and Equipment Accounts of the LGU. 

Once the one-time cleansing is done, we recommended to the Management to:

1. Instruct the General Services Officer to:

a. Facilitate the procurement of supplies and PPE by issuing a PR at the beginning of the quarter in accordance with the Annual Procurement Plan of the LGU to avoid unavailability of stocks and consequent delay in the operations of the end-user units;
b. Secure the Purchase Orders from the BAC and monitor the delivery of the supplies and PPE;
c. Maintain an updated stock cards for supplies, and property cards for PPE;
d. Reconcile its records with the records of the Municipal Accountant’s Office; and
e. Submit to the Office of the Auditor the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) and Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) within the reglementary period of submission.
2. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Maintain an updated Property, Plant and Equipment Cards for each category of assets; and Real Property Ledger Cards for Land; and 
b. Record and disclose the PPE based on the recognition criteria and disclosure requirement set under IPSAS 17 and the Government Auditing Manual (GAM) for LGUs.
3. Undertake the annual physical inventory count for the succeeding years.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to:
Identify the existence of the public infrastructures recorded in the RPI in coordination with the General Service Office (GSO) and Engineering Office;
After identifying the existence of the public infrastructures, prepare the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) through the Accounting Office and make sure to completely fill-up the following information:
Description of the infrastructure asset (type, location, parameters, etc.)
Date of acquisition
Estimated Useful Life in accordance with the guidelines set by the LGU based on its experience
Rate of Depreciation in accordance with existing accounting rules
Residual value in accordance with existing accounting rules
Accumulated Depreciation as calculated based on the given date of acquisition, estimated useful life, rate of depreciation and residual value
After preparing the individual PPELC, record the infrastructure assets in the appropriate infrastructure asset accounts in the books of accounts of the LGU.

We recommended to the management to do the following:
Coordinate with the PGB in order to reconcile the variance for the project, KKK, Local Government Special Fund;
For non-capitalizable expenditures, debit the appropriate expenditure accounts upon disbursement, and debit the related liability account and credit the appropriate income account once liquidation is made, and
Promptly liquidate the expended financial assistance with the PGB.

We recommended to the management, through the LFC, to classify its projects according to the proper expense class: Capital Outlay (CO) for those that are expected to bring economic benefits to the LGU for more than one (1) year, and Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) for those that are not expected to bring economic benefits to the LGU for more than one (1) year.

We recommended to the management, through the Accounting Office, to review thoroughly the completeness of the documents supporting the claims for progress billings and coordinate with the concerned units and contractors for the preparation and attachments of the lacking documents to every progress billing.
We recommended to the management to properly identify the contract effectivity based on the above-provisions and to completely fill-up the Statement of Time Elapsed.

We recommended to the management to demand from the contractor a copy of the bond endorsement from the insurance company extending the coverage period of the seven (7) warranty securities with insufficient coverage period identified in Annex H and submit the warranty security of the project, Rehabilitation of Multi-Purpose Hall (Tanod Outpost, Daycare, BNS & BSPO Building).

We recommended to the management to match the amount of the replenishments with the expenditures made from the PCF and to refrain refunding or closing the PCF at year-end and every cash examination cut-off.
We recommended to the management to remit the unexpended PDAF in the amount of P2,025,157.30 to the Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended to the management to properly program and appropriate non-capitalizable and capitalizable expenditures in the MOOE and CO, respectively, and to follow proper procedures in utilizing the same.

We recommended to the management through the HRMO to submit a certificate stating the following:
For payment of SRI:
That the personnel have rendered at least a total or an aggregate of four (4) months of satisfactory services as of November 30, 2021;
That the employees have not received any additional year-end benefit in FY 2021 over and above the benefits authorized under Republic Act (RA) No. 6686, as amended by RA NO. 8441; and
That the personnel have not been found guilty of any administrative or criminal cases by final and executory judgment in FY 2021 or if found guilty, the penalty imposed is only a reprimand.
For payment of Gratuity Pay:
That the COS and JO workers have rendered a total or an aggregate of at least four (4) months of actual satisfactory performance of services, as stipulated in their respective contracts, as of December 15, 2021; and
That their contracts of are still effective as of December 15, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to direct the Municipal Accounting Office to adhere to the prescribed accounting entries pertaining the use of the previous years’ LDRRMF in order to have fair presentation of the financial transactions of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2019 | None.
observations.reason:
As of December 31, 2020, the TF has P6,254,189.27 under the line item, “Transfers, Assistance and Subsidy” in the Statement of Financial Performance.

The Municipal Accountant reasoned that there is no compliance with the audit recommendation because there is confusion between the directive given by the COA Regional Office No. 10 and this audit recommendation. She said that during the conversion to NGAS, trainings conducted by COA Regional Office No. 10 required the Accountants to reflect the expenses charged against the LDRRMF in the Statement of Financial Performance. She expressed her concern that the Accountants of the LGUs of Bukidnon also have different interpretations as to the proper treatment of the expenses. Thus, she asked for a final resolution from the COA and will adhere to whatever will be resolved thereafter. She also suggested that the COA conduct trainings for all LGU Accountants as to how to properly close the LDRRMF in the trust fund in order for the uniform application of the procedures in the Province of Bukidnon.

The audit team, informed the Municipal Accountant that proforma accounting entries are provided in COA Circular No. 2012-002. However, due to the confusion with the directive given during the trainings at the COA Regional Office No. 10, the audit team will raise the matter with the Supervising Auditor and the audit group as to the proper treatment of the expenses, its proper presentation in the Statement of Financial Performance, and for the uniform application of the procedures by the LGUs of Bukidnon.

observations.managementAction:
AAR 2019 | As to the contention that the P210,000.00 was not duly supported with an appropriation ordinance, please be informed that there is an Appropriation Ordinance #08 series of 2019 dated December 20, 2019 and approved on December 23, 2019 under the Supplemental Budget No.2 CY 2019 of the Economic Enterprise.
The PRAISE Committee with the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan decided to grant such benefit to all employees recognizing the efforts of its department/individual employee and contributed to the achievements of the organization as a whole. The management is asking for consideration for these benefits as we are giving an amount lower than the allowable CNA benefits which the LGU did not give. The management is also exercising due prudence in the utilization of government resources as we still have enough surplus to fund for the needs of the LGU in the amount of P18,347,921.81 at year-end. Had the management, did not exercise such prudence, they can grant a higher amount knowing they have such savings but opted to grant just and fair amount of P20,000.00/each as a reward of the collective efforts of all the employees of the LGU.
In regards to the copies of the Program on Awards and Incentives for Service Excellence (PRAISE) that was labeled “hybrid” of the old CSC-approved PRAISE and the Re-organized PRAISE, there are actually two (2) different copies of PRAISE submitted to your office:
(a) The approved Program on Awards and Incentives for Service Excellence (PRAISE) approved on February 8, 2011 signed by the former Local Chief Executive was approved by the Civil Service Commission on March 9, 2011. Attached with this copy is the Resolution No. 60 s. 2011 of the Sangguniang Bayan Office dated May 4, 2011 also signed by the previous officials of the Municipality. This is to justify that the Municipality of Kibawe has had an approved PRAISE from the CSC. As for the last page missing its page number, it was honestly the file left by the former HR before the present HR has taken the position.
(b) The other one is the Re-organized Program on Awards and Incentives for Service Excellence (PRAISE) done after the change of administration during the second half of the year 2019, signed on July 2, 2019. This PRAISE as well as the PRAISE Committee was approved and signed by the new members of the current administration. Unfortunately, as it was already nearing the end the year (2019), approval from the CSC is not possible. It was however adopted by the Sangguniang Bayan Office on November 18, 2019 as Resolution No. 114 s. 2019 signed by the new set of officials. As of this date, approval of the Re-organized PRAISE is still pending approval of the CSC.
With this, there might have been an honest error in the submission of the files asked from the HR Office that caused these two different PRAISE copies to be labeled “hybrid”. Attached herewith are the PRAISE approved on 2011 and the Re-organized PRAISE made during the new administration.

observations.reason: There are still some contracts which were submitted beyond the prescribed period due to the lack of sufficient manpower in the BAC, although, the Municipality is already doing its efforts to submit within the reglementary period.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Local Chief Executive initiate the proper implementation of the prioritized programs, projects, and activities funded from the 20% LDF. Prepare plans and programs showing the specific activities, time of start and completion, identifying officials/employees responsible for the implementation of every project or activity. Cause closer coordination among the officials/employees involved in planning, execution, and monitoring to maximize utilization and speed up its implementation to ensure that intended beneficiaries could enjoy optimum benefits at the earliest possible time.
observations.managementAction: The Management strives to coordinate with officials and employees directly involved in the planning, execution and monitoring of the projects to be implemented.
observations.reason: The Engineering Office lacks manpower especially to implement the projects within the planned period of accomplishment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to direct the Municipal Accountant to duly record the monthly depreciation expense for all PPE items that are already available for use unless the PPE item has been fully depreciated.
observations.managementAction: During the Exit Conference last March 12, 2019, the Management informed the Audit Team that starting March 2019, they will be computing the depreciation of the Municipality's newly acquired PPE on a monthly basis. However, depreciation for its previously acquired PPE will still be computed and recognized annually.
observations.reason: The Management failed to compute and recognize the depeciation of its PPE on a monthly basis during CY 2018 due to the lack of sufficient staff to handle the job.

observations.recommendation: b. Require the Budget Office to thoroughly evaluate the submitted PPMPs in terms of amount and coherence with the proposed budget of the LGU
observations.managementAction: b. The BAC was tasked to evaluate the PPMPs in accordance with the proposed budget of the LGU.
observations.reason: Some items included in the Annual Budget were not present in the PPMPs of the respective offices especially the infrastructure projects implemented during the current year due to the failure of the BAC to properly evaluate each PPMP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Issue a memorandum that the regular physical count of Inventories, PPE and Biological Assets be conducted per department/office to be submitted for consolidation to the GSO in order to fast-track the conduct of the physical count;
Direct the GSO to submit the required physical count reports to the Office of the Resident Auditor within the prescribed period of submission in order to allow this office to conduct a timely verification of accounts; and
Direct the GSO to furnish the Accounting Office with the physical count reports in order to facilitate the reconciliation of the property records with the accounting records.

We recommended and the management agreed to require:
The Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) furnish the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) a certified list of name of the tax payers and the amount due and collectible for the year 2019 and the previous years;
The MAO to establish the Real Property Tax Receivable and Deferred Real Property Tax Income every beginning of the year.
The MAO to record the delinquent real property taxes in the Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax accounts and make adjustments to the Deferred Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax Income; and
The MAO to record the collections using its appropriate accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the proper disclosure in its Notes to the Financial Statements for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2018 on the liquidation of the Provincial Fund last October 31, 2018 amounting to P10,000,000.00, which was transferred from the Provincial Government of Bukidnon for the Construction of Market Stalls and that the same could not be recorded in the books of accounts of the Municipality as a debit to Due to LGU account since the project is not yet fully completed and that the liquidation of the initial fund transfer is necessary for the release of additional financial assistance for the completion of the aforementioned project.

We recommended and the management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the appropriate subsidiary ledgers of the Cash Local Treasury Account, for proper observance of the General Accounting Plan for Local Government Units (LGUs) as prescribed by the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS).
We recommended and the Management agreed to require the MTO to observe the frequency of the release of the barangay share of the real property tax it collected.

We recommended and the management agreed to:
Enter into a written job order contract or pakyaw labor contract for the labor component of their projects to be implemented by administration in accordance with existing rules and regulations, and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents before processing the disbursement vouchers and payrolls for the payment of labor services.

We recommended and the management agreed to:
Instruct the GSO to:
design and adopt procedures in the monitoring of undelivered goods and supplies;
compute and deduct liquidated for delayed delivery of goods and supplies; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to review the correctness of the computation and withhold the amount of liquidated damages for delayed deliveries of goods and supplies.

We recommended and the management agreed to:
Instruct the BAC Secretariat to prepare the Notice to Proceed within three (3) calendar days from the approval of the contract/purchase order and ensure that the same contains the approval of the Mayor and the signature of the supplier’s authorized representative as well as the date of receipt by the supplier;
Instruct the BAC Secretariat to completely fill-up the purchase orders, most importantly the delivery date; and
Instruct the GSO to indicate the date of acceptance on the AIR.
We recommended and the management agreed to require the Accountant to set up a petty cash fund for the payment of its petty expenses in an imprest system.

We recommended and the Management agreed to request the contractor to post a Warranty Security before the release of the Certificate of Final Acceptance of project by the LGU in accordance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended and the management agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer exercise the powers provided in Book II Title II of RA 7160 to enforce the collection of the Real Property Taxes.
We further recommended the management to:
To serve the notices via mail with return cards to ensure its receipt by the taxpayers especially in areas where the house to house collections cannot be made; and
To reconcile its computation with the taxpayers own computation of the RPT.

We recommended the management to direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Compute the depreciation based on the depreciable amount after deducting the five percent (5%) residual value and using the straight line method as required in PPSAS 17 unless the LGU decides that a different percentage for the residual value and a different depreciation method is appropriate.
observations.managementAction: a. The Medical Equipment Account had a negative value as of December 31, 2017 because its ending balance for the calendar year 2015 was not  carried forward to the next accounting period. The Accounting Office, made an adjusting entry to correct the error.

observations.recommendation: b. Properly observe the required five percent (5%) residual value across all classes of items in the PPE for uniformity, unless there is sufficient reason for the LGU to apply a different percentage in computing for the residual value of a particular item or class of items in the PPE based on the nature of its operations.
observations.managementAction: b. The Accounting Office recomputed its depreciation expense for its PPE in order to observe the correct 5% residual value as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to properly disclose the changes in the carrying amount of the items in PPE in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management disclosed the changes in the carrying amount of the items in its PPE Account per Note 8 of its Notes to Financial Statements as of December 31, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to direct the Municipal Accountant to provide the necessary information relating to the accounts as herein mentioned and submit it to the Office of the COA Auditor for its inclusion in the LGU’s Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office made the required disclosures in its Notes to Financial Statements as of December 31, 2018 for the following identified accounts: PPE Accounts, Due to Other NGAs, and Due to LGUs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to direct the BAC to regularly prepare the PMR in accordance with the provisions of RA 9184 and in the format prescribed by the GPPB.
observations.managementAction: The BAC prepared its PMR for CY 2018. A copy was submitted to the Resident COA Auditor's Office.

observations.recommendation: a. Require the End-User Units to prepare the PPMPs in accordance with Section 7 of RA 9184 using the suggested format by the GPPB duly posted in its website.
observations.managementAction: a. For CY 2018, the PPMPs were prepared by the respective departments except for items classified under Non-Office and 20% LDF.
observations.reason: The preparation of the PPMPs of items under Non-Office and LDF was not delegated to a specific office by the Management, hence, no PPMPs were prepared for these items.

observations.recommendation: c. Require the BAC through the BAC Secretariat to consolidate the PPMPs and prepare the APP in accordance to the applicable provisions of RA 9184 using the prescribed format under GPPB Circular No. 07-2015 and GPPB Resolution No. 20-2015 dated July 16, 2015.
observations.managementAction: c. For CY 2018, the APP was prepared based on the submitted PPMPs except for items classified under Non-Office and 20% LDF.
observations.reason: The items classified under the Non-Office and LDF did not have PPMPs, hence, were not included in the consolidation for the preparation of the APP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management to require the:

a. BAC to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 in the procurement of fuel.
observations.managementAction: a. Starting CY 2018, the Agency now procures its gasoline, oil and lubricants through public bidding in accordance to RA 9184. Pre-procurement and Pre-Bid Activities have also been conducted as part of the bidding process.

observations.recommendation: b. End-User Units to avoid withdrawals of fuel from supplier gasoline stations without first securing an approved purchase order for such withdrawal.
observations.managementAction: b. The End-Users are no longer allowed to get their fuel  from the gasoline stations without going through the proper procurement process.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management to require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Record and obligate expenses in the year that they are incurred, otherwise, obligate first the entire amount on the purchase order prior to the approval of the same.
observations.managementAction: a. Fuel expenses incurred but not yet paid as of year-end were already obligated during the current before the accounts were closed.

observations.recommendation: b. Record prior year expenses in the accounts payable, provided, that such expenses were duly obligated in the year they are incurred.
observations.managementAction: b. Accounts payable for the unpaid gasoline, oil  and  lubricants expenses incurred during the current year has been set up at year-end  for the payments of such expenses in the following accounting period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to direct the Division Heads to require the assigned drivers of their respective government motor vehicles to properly accomplish their daily trip tickets and to submit Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption per Vehicle in the correct format.
observations.managementAction: Starting CY 2018, daily trip tickets were accomplished by the drivers. However, no Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption were submitted to the Resident COA Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to direct the Municipal Accountant to withhold 5% Final VAT and 1% Income Tax from fuel expense billings and duly remit it to the authorized internal revenue officer / agent.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office withholds and remits to the BIR the proper taxes from the fuel billings.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management to:

a. Implement an adequate monitoring system that tracks the loan payables of its employees from authorized agencies.
observations.managementAction: a. The Accounting Office monitored the net take home pay of its employee to ensure that it follows the required minimum amount.

observations.recommendation: b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from approving the loan applications of those employees whose net take pays are below the mandated minimum net take home pay.
observations.managementAction: b. Starting CY 2018, employee loan applications were not allowed or approved if their net take home pay is below the required minimum.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to advise the Municipal Accountant/ payroll clerk to follow the rules under Section 7 of R.A. No. 9679 for purposes of properly computing the government share as well as personal share of the HDMF contributions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the management to:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all unliquidated savings from trust fund projects to the Bureau of National Treasury in the event where the source agency of the related fund transfer is unidentifiable.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office remitted the unliquidated savings to its source agencies.
observations.reason: Some unliquidated savings especially those without identified source agencies remained in the Municipality's accounts.

observations.recommendation: b. Advise and direct all concerned officials and employees to abide with the fundamental principles governing LGUs as laid down in R.A. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Management strives to abide with the principles under RA 7160.

observations.reason: Lacking effective monitoring.

observations.recommendation:

a. Require the Council to reevaluate its plans and programs and provide realistic time frames for the implementation of such.

Cause closer coordination among the officials/employees involved in planning, execution, and monitoring to maximize utilization and speed up its implementation to ensure that intended beneficiaries could enjoy optimum benefits at the earliest possible time.
observations.managementAction: In the year 2016, 15 out of 18 projects or 83% were already implement- ted.
observations.reason: Three projects are still not implemented as at year end.
